# Illustrative Application: SEU Sensitivity in LLM Decisions {#sec-application .unnumbered}

## 7.1 Why include an application here {#sec-app-why .unnumbered}

The methodology of §§[2](#sec-abstract-model)--[6](#sec-m1-implementation) is
only useful insofar as it does real evaluative work on real choice data. This
section runs the full workflow end-to-end on a $2\times2$ factorial design --- two
LLMs crossed with two task families --- to demonstrate three things: (i) the
workflow scales from the $N \approx 100$ simulated choices per condition of
§§[4](#sec-m0-implementation)/[6](#sec-m1-implementation) to $N \approx 300$
real-LLM choices per condition without redesign; (ii) prior recalibration is a
routine, principled per-study step rather than a redesign of the model; and (iii)
the framework can detect a structured comparative effect when one is present and
decline to support one when it is not. Point (iii) is the methodological-value
claim: an evaluation framework that *always* reports an effect would be a
scoreboard, not an instrument.

::: {.callout-important appearance="simple"}
**Reading guide (construct-validity layering; read first).** Three distinct
claims must not be conflated, and this layering frames everything below:

1. **Within-model consistency** --- the model fits the data adequately
   (validated in §[7.4](#sec-app-validation)).
2. **Comparative claims under shared design** --- $\alpha$ differs systematically
   across conditions that share prior, features, and choice model (the
   §[7.5](#sec-app-results) reading).
3. **Absolute claims about EU rationality** --- an LLM's $\alpha$ value certifies
   a context-free rationality level.

**Only layers 1 and 2 are licensed by this paper.** §[7.6.3](#sec-app-limits)
deepens this; it is stated here so the reader reaches the §[7.5](#sec-app-results)
headline already holding it.
:::

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
**Reporting template (applied uniformly to all four cells).** Every LLM $\times$
task cell is reported with the *same* quantities, to pre-empt cherry-picking:
per-condition $\alpha$ posterior medians with 90% CIs; the draw-wise global slope
$\Delta\alpha/\Delta T$ median, 90% CI, and $P(\text{slope} < 0)$;
$P(\text{strict monotone decrease across all levels})$; and the cross-LLM and
cross-task comparison probabilities. No cell omits a quantity.
:::

## 7.2 The $2\times2$ design {#sec-app-design .unnumbered}

**LLMs.** GPT-4o (OpenAI) and Claude 3.5 Sonnet (Anthropic).

**Task families.** (i) *Insurance claims triage* with $K = 3$ consequences
(forward / hold / decline); and (ii) *Ellsberg-style urn gambles* with $K = 4$
consequences (\$0, \$1, \$2, \$3 payouts) over a 30-gamble pool organized into
three ambiguity tiers (Tier 1 unambiguous, Tier 2 moderately ambiguous, Tier 3
high-ambiguity in the spirit of @ellsberg1961).

**Per cell.** Five sampling-temperature conditions; $M \approx 100$ base problems
$\times$ 3 position-counterbalanced presentations $= 300$ choices per condition
(constants in Appendix D.5). Temperature grids differ by provider:
GPT-4o $\{0.0, 0.3, 0.7, 1.0, 1.5\}$; Claude $\{0.0, 0.2, 0.5, 0.8, 1.0\}$
(Anthropic-API range constraint). The unequal grids complicate direct
slope-*magnitude* comparison across LLMs, though not the qualitative reading; we
flag this again at §[7.5.2a](#sec-app-results).

**Feature pipeline.** *Insurance:* two-stage prompting (per-claim LLM assessment,
then choice over assessments); assessment text embedded via
`text-embedding-3-small`; pooled-PCA projection to $D = 32$. *Ellsberg:*
alternatives carry explicit ball counts and payouts, so the feature
representation is the gamble's stated structure --- no LLM-mediated text features.
The insurance application therefore measures the *whole assessment-and-choice
pipeline*, not the choice stage in isolation; the Ellsberg application strips this
layer (we return to this asymmetry at §[7.6.5](#sec-app-limits)). Position
counterbalancing addresses a position-bias problem identified in an earlier
pilot; unparseable responses are recorded as missing rather than coerced to a
default, and missing rates are negligible across all 20 conditions.

## 7.3 The model fit (`m_01`) {#sec-app-model .unnumbered}

The fitted model is `m_01`: structurally identical to `m_0` of
§[4](#sec-m0-implementation) but with a $\mathrm{Lognormal}(\mu, \sigma)$ prior on
$\alpha$ calibrated to the application's design and consequence space.

**Prior calibration anchor.** The calibration target is the prior predictive
SEU-maximizer selection rate (fraction of simulated problems on which the
simulated agent picks the EU-maximal available alternative). The foundational
$\mathrm{Lognormal}(0,1)$ prior of §[4.1](#sec-m0-spec) places excessive mass on near-random
regimes and, at the application's $R$ and $K$, on tails that cause softmax
overflow under the study design. A grid search over twelve Lognormal
hyperparameter pairs selects $\mathrm{Lognormal}(3.0, 0.75)$ for the insurance
task ($K = 3$): median $\alpha \approx 20$, 90% interval $\approx [5.5, 67]$,
implied SEU-max rate $\approx 78\%$. For the Ellsberg task ($K = 4$) the prior is
*re-recalibrated* by the same procedure, because the random-choice baseline
shifts from $1/3$ to $1/4$ and the $\alpha$ corresponding to any given SEU-max
rate shifts with it.

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
**Only the prior is recalibrated, not the likelihood.** The $\alpha$-identifiability
proposition of §[3.3](#sec-alpha-from-eta)/§[5.4](#sec-m1-alpha) and the
weakly-informed $(\beta,\delta)$ finding of §[3.4](#sec-bd-weak) carry over
unchanged from `m_0` to `m_01`. The application inherits the identifiability story
of §§[3](#sec-m0-identifiability) and [5](#sec-m1-identifiability) without
re-derivation. This foundational-vs-application prior distinction is the one
flagged in §[4.1](#sec-m0-spec): same likelihood, different $\alpha$ prior.
:::

## 7.4 Validation at the application's scale {#sec-app-validation .unnumbered}

The workflow of §§[4.2](#sec-m0-prior)--[4.4](#sec-m0-sbc) is re-run at the
application's scale ($M = 300$, $K = 3$ or $4$, $D = 32$, $R = 30$, 20 recovery
iterations per cell).

**Parameter recovery for $\alpha$.** Anchored on relative metrics, because true
$\alpha$ lies on a wide multiplicative scale under the calibrated prior: relative
bias within $\pm 10\%$ of the mean true value, relative RMSE well below 25%, and
90% CI coverage at nominal. As throughout, $(\beta, \delta)$ recovery remains weak
--- the §[3](#sec-m0-identifiability) picture is unchanged --- but the
cross-condition comparison is a claim about $\alpha$, and $\alpha$ is fit for
purpose.

**SBC for $\alpha$.** SBC validates the *(prior, likelihood, sampler)* triple and
is conditionally independent of which agent generated the held-out empirical data.

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
**Lemma (SBC inheritance).** If two fitted studies use the same Stan model, the
same prior hyperparameters, and the same feature pipeline, an SBC result
established for one transfers to the other without re-running. *Application:* SBC
for $\alpha$ under $\mathrm{Lognormal}(3.0, 0.75)$ and the insurance likelihood is
established once in the GPT-4o $\times$ insurance report and inherited validly by
the Claude $\times$ insurance study. The Ellsberg studies differ in $K$ and in the
$\alpha$ prior and so receive **fresh** SBC under the $K = 4$ recalibrated prior.
:::

**MCMC diagnostics.** Across all 20 factorial fits, $\alpha$ --- the parameter the
§[7.5](#sec-app-results) temperature analysis rests on --- is **never**
$\hat{R}$-flagged, and ESS is satisfactory in every fit, so the per-condition
$\alpha$ posteriors are a sound basis for the cross-condition comparison.
Divergent transitions are negligible ($\leq 0.15\%$ per fit, $\leq 6/4000$; 34
total), though spread across 12/20 fits in both tasks and both providers rather
than confined to the highest GPT-4o temperatures. $\hat{R} > 1.01$ appears in
4/20 fits, **confined to the weakly-informed $\beta/\delta$ and the
high-dimensional per-trial nuisance latents** ($\eta$, $\psi$, $\upsilon$) in the
harder $K = 4$ GPT-4o $\times$ Ellsberg fits (plus one Claude $\times$ insurance
fit, $\eta$ only). This *corroborates* the §[3.4](#sec-bd-weak)/§[6.4](#sec-m1-recovery)
weakly-informed $(\beta,\delta)$ finding --- the poorly-mixing parameters are
exactly the ones recovery flags as weakly informed --- and is not a defect in
$\alpha$ inference.

**Posterior predictive checks.** Three complementary summaries (log-likelihood,
modal-choice frequency, mean predicted probability of the chosen alternative)
yield posterior predictive $p$-values in $[0.32, 0.66]$ across all 20 fits (60/60
in band, mean $\approx 0.46$) --- no evidence of systematic misfit. PPC adequacy
is a *necessary* (not sufficient) condition for the per-condition $\alpha$
posteriors to be a credible basis for the cross-condition comparison; absent it,
the comparison should be set aside.

## 7.5 Results --- the $2\times2$ cross-LLM $\times$ cross-task picture {#sec-app-results .unnumbered}

### 7.5.1 GPT-4o $\times$ insurance {#sec-app-gpt4o-insurance .unnumbered}

Posterior medians of $\alpha$ are highest at $T = 0.0$ and lowest at $T = 1.5$,
intermediate temperatures monotone-ish in between. The draw-wise global slope
$\Delta\alpha/\Delta T$ has median $\approx -31$, 90% CI $\approx [-66, -8]$,
$P(\text{slope} < 0) \approx 0.99$. The probability of strict monotonic decrease
across all five levels is only $\approx 0.12$ (driven by overlap between
$T = 0.3$ and $T = 0.7$); collapsing those two raises it to $\approx 0.38$. The
headline *directional* claim is well-supported; fine-grained adjacent-step
orderings are not.

### 7.5.2 Claude $\times$ insurance {#sec-app-claude-insurance .unnumbered}

The framework **does not detect** a monotonic $\alpha$--temperature pattern
(note the careful phrasing: *does not detect*, not *establishes the absence of*).
Posterior medians are $\approx \{74, 55, 77, 74, 57\}$ at $\{0.0, 0.2, 0.5, 0.8,
1.0\}$. Global slope: median $\approx -3.6$, 90% CI $\approx [-54, 39]$,
$P(\text{slope} < 0) \approx 0.56$; $P(\text{strict monotone decrease}) < 0.01$.
The oscillation in the medians is consistent with posterior noise around a roughly
flat function, not a substantive non-monotonic response.

::: {.callout-warning appearance="simple"}
**Minimum-detectable-effect (calibrating the Claude null).** The design could
resolve a temperature-on-$\alpha$ slope at $P(\text{slope} < 0) \geq 0.95$ only if
its magnitude were $|\Delta\alpha/\Delta T| \gtrsim 36$ $\alpha$-units per unit
temperature (three independent estimators agree: analytic-Gaussian 36.3,
empirical-quantile 34.7, constant-CV Monte-Carlo 36.0; $\approx 41$--$43$ at the
stricter $P \geq 0.975$). That floor is $\approx 12\times$ the observed
$|\text{slope}|$ ($\approx 2.9$) and $\approx 0.53$ of the grand-mean $\alpha$
($\approx 67.5$) --- an end-to-end $\alpha$ change of $\approx 36$ over
$T \in [0, 1]$, roughly halving $\alpha$. So the Claude null is *no effect at the
achievable resolution*, **not** a measured zero; even a GPT-4o-sized slope
($\approx 31$, full-grid reference) sits below this floor ($0.86\times$). See the
power curve (Fig 7.5.2). This calibration prevents the §[7.6.1](#sec-app-limits)
"declines to support" framing from being read as a positive no-effect claim.
:::

### 7.5.2a Cross-LLM comparison --- pattern first, number second {#sec-app-cross-llm .unnumbered}

A formal cross-study comparison gives $P(\text{GPT-4o slope} < \text{Claude
slope}) = 0.817$ on the full grids; restricting GPT-4o to Claude's $T \leq 1.0$
ceiling (re-summarizing with $T = 1.5$ dropped) gives $0.824$ --- the directional
LLM contrast is **robust to the unequal-grid confound** (GPT-4o spans
$T \in [0.0, 1.5]$; Claude spans $[0.0, 1.0]$). We report the Claude-grid-restricted
number alongside the full-grid one and make the *qualitative pattern*
(monotone-decline vs.\ no-monotone) the headline, not the numeric inequality. Both
between-LLM probabilities ($\sim 0.82$) are weaker than GPT-4o's own within-cell
$P(\text{slope} < 0) > 0.98$ because the cells are fitted independently.
"Temperature" is not the same instrument across providers (§[7.6.2](#sec-app-limits)(a)).

### 7.5.3 GPT-4o $\times$ Ellsberg {#sec-app-gpt4o-ellsberg .unnumbered}

The monotonic decline of $\alpha$ with temperature is reproduced on the Ellsberg
task. $\alpha$ medians $\{110.4, 106.9, 99.5, 84.0, 52.2\}$ at $T = \{0.0, 0.3,
0.7, 1.0, 1.5\}$ (90% CIs $[74.4, 167.2]$ / $[72.6, 163.8]$ / $[65.4, 154.6]$ /
$[57.1, 126.1]$ / $[35.5, 80.3]$); global slope $\Delta\alpha/\Delta T$ median
$-38.4$, 90% CI $[-72.1, -10.0]$, $P(\text{slope} < 0) \, 0.984$;
$P(\text{strict monotone} \downarrow) \, 0.090$ (a noisy decline, not step-wise).

### 7.5.4 Claude $\times$ Ellsberg {#sec-app-claude-ellsberg .unnumbered}

No monotonic $\alpha$--temperature pattern. $\alpha$ medians $\{85.4, 56.1, 82.3,
53.0, 66.4\}$ at $T = \{0.0, 0.2, 0.5, 0.8, 1.0\}$ (90% CIs $[58.9, 127.2]$ /
$[38.2, 84.1]$ / $[52.3, 135.3]$ / $[36.0, 80.5]$ / $[45.3, 99.8]$) --- the
medians oscillate rather than decline; global slope $\Delta\alpha/\Delta T$ median
$-18.8$, 90% CI $[-65.3, 24.5]$, $P(\text{slope} < 0) \, 0.766$;
$P(\text{strict monotone} \downarrow) \, 0.0085$. As with Claude $\times$
insurance, this is *does not detect*, not *establishes absence* (cf.\ the
§[7.5.2](#sec-app-claude-insurance) MDE discipline).

### 7.5.5 The $2\times2$ reading {#sec-app-2x2 .unnumbered}

| | **Insurance** | **Ellsberg** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **GPT-4o** | slope $-31$ $[-66, -8]$, $P_{<0}\,0.99$ | slope $-38.4$ $[-72.1, -10.0]$, $P_{<0}\,0.98$ |
| **Claude 3.5** | slope $-3.6$ $[-54, 39]$, $P_{<0}\,0.56$ | slope $-18.8$ $[-65.3, 24.5]$, $P_{<0}\,0.77$ |

: Per-cell global-slope $\Delta\alpha/\Delta T$ posteriors (median, 90% CI,
$P(\text{slope}<0)$). GPT-4o shows the temperature--$\alpha$ decline in both task
families; Claude shows it in neither. {#tbl-2x2}

In this $2\times2$, the LLM $\times$ task factorial isolates a clean **LLM
effect**: GPT-4o exhibits the temperature--$\alpha$ relationship across both task
families, Claude exhibits it in neither. *In this $2\times2$,* task family does not
appear to be the relevant axis; the LLM does. With only two task families and two
LLMs this is a **pattern statement, not a factorial generalization**
(§[7.6.2](#sec-app-limits)(d)). It is best presented as a $2\times2$ forest plot of
the per-cell global-slope posteriors.

## 7.6 What the application demonstrates --- and what it does not {#sec-app-limits .unnumbered}

### 7.6.1 What it demonstrates {#sec-app-demonstrates .unnumbered}

(i) The full workflow of §§[4](#sec-m0-implementation) and
[6](#sec-m1-implementation) runs end-to-end on real LLM choice data, at the
application's scale, with all validation diagnostics clean. (ii) Prior
recalibration via the prior predictive SEU-maximizer rate is a routine, principled
per-study step. (iii) The framework detects a structured comparative effect in two
of four LLM $\times$ task cells (GPT-4o, both tasks) and **does not detect** one in
the other two (Claude, both tasks). This last point is the methodological-value
claim --- but stated carefully: the Claude cells are *inconclusive nulls*
(§[7.5.2](#sec-app-claude-insurance)), so the licensed claim is "an instrument
that does not manufacture an effect where its own diagnostics cannot resolve one,"
**not** "an instrument that certifies the absence of an effect." The
minimum-detectable-effect statement of §[7.5.2](#sec-app-claude-insurance) is what
keeps this claim resting on calibrated resolution rather than an unsupported null.

### 7.6.2 What it does not demonstrate {#sec-app-not-demonstrate .unnumbered}

**(a) That sampling temperature is a portable behavioral instrument.** The Claude
null does not reproduce the GPT-4o pattern under the same task and choice model, so
"temperature" as an experimental lever travels poorly across providers. **(b) That
GPT-4o is "more rational" than Claude in any context-free sense.** The licensed
reading of $\alpha$ here is a *within-design comparative* one, not an
absolute-rationality ranking. **(c) Anything about ambiguity aversion in the
Ellsberg study.** The SEU+softmax model is, by assumption, of the wrong form to
distinguish ambiguity-driven choice from EU-driven choice; testing for ambiguity
aversion requires a model with explicit ambiguity-attitude parameters (e.g.,
@gilboa1989; $\alpha$-MEU à la @ghirardato2004), and pooling across the three
ambiguity tiers as the application does would yield a mixture rather than identify
a mechanism. Tier-stratified $\alpha$ is named as future work, not a paper claim.
**(d) That LLM identity is the only relevant axis in general.** With only two task
families and two LLMs, the $2\times2$ supports a pattern statement, not a factorial
generalization.

### 7.6.3 Construct-validity layering (reaffirmed) {#sec-app-construct-validity .unnumbered}

The three-layer reading guide of §[7.1](#sec-app-why) --- (1) within-model
consistency, (2) comparative claims under shared design, (3) absolute claims about
EU rationality, with only (1) and (2) licensed --- is the single most important
reading instruction for this section. The §[7.5](#sec-app-results) cross-condition
contrast is a layer-(2) claim; it is *not* a layer-(3) certification of either
LLM's context-free rationality.

### 7.6.4 Ellsberg: do not adjudicate the normative question {#sec-app-ellsberg-normative .unnumbered}

The Ellsberg stimuli carry historical weight that does not reduce to the
behavioral "ambiguity aversion" reading: alternative readings include Ellsberg's
own normative defense [@ellsberg1961; @ellsberg2001] and Levi's reading as a
violation of the completeness axiom rather than the sure-thing principle
[@levi1980; @levi1986]. A study that takes SEU as its *measurement device* cannot
use the resulting estimates to vindicate or refute SEU as a *normative* standard.
(See @camerer1992 and @trautmann2015 for the broader behavioral-ambiguity
literature.) The paper's contribution lies elsewhere.

### 7.6.5 The insurance application measures the whole pipeline {#sec-app-pipeline-caveat .unnumbered}

The insurance application does not isolate a single cognitive stage, and it is
worth being precise about what its $\alpha$ actually measures. Each insurance
trial is produced by a *composed system*: a first LLM call assesses the
free-text claim, a second renders that assessment into the structured terms the
analysis consumes, an embedding model maps the resulting text to a vector, and a
PCA step reduces it to the feature space the choice model reads
(§[7.2](#sec-app-design)). The fitted sensitivity $\alpha$ is a property of that
whole assembly --- the two LLM calls together with the deterministic feature
pipeline --- and not of the choice stage taken on its own. The estimand is
therefore the SEU sensitivity *of the pipeline as configured*, and the
temperature lever acts on every LLM-mediated component of it at once. This is the
appropriate object of study for the methodological claim --- the workflow
recovers a well-calibrated $\alpha$ for a realistic end-to-end system --- but it
means that a within-cell change in $\alpha$ cannot, by itself, be attributed to
any one stage. The GPT-4o insurance monotonicity is thus consistent with
temperature affecting (i) the assessment stage, (ii) the choice stage, or
(iii) both.

The Ellsberg replication is what narrows this down, because it removes the
assessment layer rather than holding it fixed. There the alternatives are stated
structurally --- explicit urns and payoffs --- so no free-text assessment or
embedding step intervenes between the stimulus and the choice, and the fitted
$\alpha$ reflects the choice stage far more directly. That the GPT-4o temperature
pattern *reproduces* under this stripped-down design shows it cannot be an
artifact of the assessment stage (i) alone: a comparable effect survives when
stage (i) is absent. The two task families therefore license different readings
--- the insurance cell speaks to the assembled pipeline, the Ellsberg cell to
something much closer to the choice stage --- and taking them together
*strengthens* the case rather than weakening it. The caveat is asymmetric: it
bounds what an insurance-only result would establish, and the Ellsberg
replication is precisely what supplies the missing isolation.

## 7.7 What the application motivates for follow-up work {#sec-app-followup .unnumbered}

**Design-induced cross-condition correlation.** The five temperature conditions in
each cell draw from a single fixed pool of $R = 30$ alternatives. Whatever is
idiosyncratic about that pool --- embedding geometry, EU spread, typical
best-vs-second-best gap --- shifts every condition's $\alpha$ estimate in a
correlated way. Fitting `m_01` independently per condition treats the five
posteriors as statistically unrelated, slightly overstating between-condition
resolution. The principled fix is the hierarchical extension `h_m01`:
$\log \alpha_c = \gamma_0 + \gamma_1 T_c + \eta_c$ with a small cell-level random
effect, which turns between-condition contrasts into estimated regression effects
on $\log \alpha$, makes the shared-pool nuisance a single estimated quantity that
cancels out of contrasts, and gives one calibrated uncertainty statement about the
temperature--sensitivity relationship.

**Companion paper.** A planned alignment-study companion paper (multi-LLM $\times$
multi-prompt factorial) will introduce `h_m01` as the analysis vehicle and apply
it to the alignment-study data. That paper is out of scope here; the present paper
references it once, as the natural next step for which `h_m01` was developed.

**Tier-stratified $\alpha$** for the Ellsberg studies, and a non-SEU comparator
(e.g., a small $\alpha$-MEU instrument) that could be paired with the SEU
instrument to triangulate ambiguity-driven vs.\ EU-driven choice, are flagged as
future work in the application reports and remain out of scope here.
